sections.title: Java Basics & Syntax
sections.content: Java is an object-oriented, class-based language designed for portability: source code is compiled into bytecode, and the JVM runs that bytecode on any platform. The standard flow is: write .java file, compile with javac, then run with java. Java is often called WORA (Write Once, Run Anywhere) because the same bytecode can run on different operating systems if a JVM is available. A Java program usually starts execution from public static void main(String[] args). public allows access from outside the class, static means main belongs to the class rather than an object, void means it returns nothing, and String[] args stores command-line arguments. A class is the basic blueprint in Java. The filename must match the public class name. Java is strongly typed, so variable types are checked at compile time. Java is also case-sensitive. Comments are //, /* */ and /** */. Packages are used to organize classes, and import is used to access classes from other packages. Important MCQ facts: bytecode is not machine code; JVM executes bytecode, while JDK includes tools like javac and JVM, JRE includes JVM plus libraries needed to run programs.

sections.title: OOP Concepts
sections.content: Java is built around four pillars of OOP. Encapsulation means bundling data and methods together and restricting direct access to data using private fields and public getters/setters. It improves security and control over data. Abstraction means hiding implementation details and showing only essential features. It is achieved using abstract classes and interfaces. Inheritance lets one class acquire properties of another using extends. It supports code reuse and represents an IS-A relationship. Java supports single inheritance for classes, but multiple inheritance of type through interfaces. Polymorphism means one interface, many forms. Compile-time polymorphism is method overloading, where methods have the same name but different parameter lists. Runtime polymorphism is method overriding, where a subclass provides its own implementation of a superclass method. Overriding requires the same method signature and a compatible return type. Final methods cannot be overridden, final classes cannot be extended, and final variables cannot be reassigned. Abstract classes can have both abstract and concrete methods; interfaces are more abstract and are used for contracts. Since Java 8, interfaces can also contain default and static methods. Important MCQ trap: overloading depends on parameter list, not return type; overriding happens at runtime through dynamic dispatch.

sections.title: Data Types & Variables
sections.content: Java has primitive and reference data types. Primitives are byte, short, int, long, float, double, char, and boolean. Reference types include arrays, classes, strings, and interfaces. Primitive sizes are fixed except boolean, whose exact storage is JVM-dependent, though conceptually it stores true/false. char is 16-bit Unicode. Integer literals are int by default; long literals should use L. Floating-point literals are double by default; float literals should use F. Type conversion can be widening (automatic, safe) or narrowing (explicit cast). Example: int to long is widening; long to int is narrowing. Variables can be local, instance, or static. Local variables do not get default values and must be initialized before use. Instance and static fields get default values. Default values are 0 for numeric types, false for boolean, and null for references. final prevents reassignment. Wrapper classes convert primitives into objects: Integer, Double, Character, and so on. Autoboxing converts primitive to wrapper automatically, and unboxing does the reverse. MCQ traps: String is not a primitive; char stores a single character; null is allowed for reference types, not primitives.

sections.title: Control Flow
sections.content: Control flow decides the order of execution. Java supports if, if-else, nested if, switch, for, while, do-while, break, continue, and return. The if statement is used for a condition with true/false branches. switch is useful when comparing one expression against many constant values. In modern Java, switch can work with int, char, String, enum, and some wrappers; older versions were more limited. Every case should usually end with break unless fall-through is intended. for loop is best when the number of iterations is known. while is a pre-test loop, so the condition is checked before entering the loop. do-while is a post-test loop, so it executes at least once. break exits the nearest loop or switch. continue skips the current loop iteration. return exits the method. Enhanced for-loop (for-each) is used to traverse arrays and collections when index-based access is not needed. MCQ traps: switch uses equality, not ranges; do-while always runs once; break in a switch stops fall-through; continue does not exit the loop, it only skips one iteration.

sections.title: Arrays & Strings
sections.content: An array is a fixed-size collection of elements of the same type. Arrays can be one-dimensional or multidimensional. In Java, arrays are objects, so they are stored on the heap. Array index starts at 0. Accessing an invalid index throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException. Array length is stored in the length field, not as a method. String is one of the most important Java topics for MCQs. String objects are immutable, meaning once created they cannot be changed. Any modification creates a new object. String literals are stored in the string pool, which helps save memory by reusing identical literals. The == operator checks reference equality, while equals() checks content equality. StringBuilder and StringBuffer are mutable. StringBuilder is faster but not synchronized; StringBuffer is synchronized and thread-safe, but slower. Common methods: length(), charAt(), substring(), indexOf(), equals(), compareTo(), toUpperCase(), toLowerCase(), trim(), replace(), split(). MCQ traps: String concatenation with + creates new objects; String is immutable; StringBuilder is preferred for repeated modifications; arrays have a fixed length once created.

sections.title: Collections Framework
sections.content: The Java Collections Framework provides ready-made data structures and algorithms. The main interfaces are List, Set, Queue, and Map. List maintains order and allows duplicates. Popular implementations are ArrayList and LinkedList. ArrayList is backed by a dynamic array and is fast for random access; LinkedList is better for frequent insertions and deletions in the middle. Set does not allow duplicate elements. HashSet does not guarantee order, while LinkedHashSet preserves insertion order. TreeSet stores elements in sorted order. Map stores key-value pairs and does not allow duplicate keys. HashMap is unordered and allows one null key; TreeMap stores keys in sorted order and usually does not allow null keys. Queue is used for FIFO behavior. PriorityQueue orders elements based on priority rather than insertion order. Iterator is used to traverse collections. fail-fast iterators detect concurrent modification and may throw ConcurrentModificationException. Generics improve type safety and reduce casting. Example: List<String> means only String values can be added. Important MCQ traps: ArrayList allows duplicates, Set does not; HashMap is not synchronized by default; TreeSet and TreeMap are sorted; Map keys must be unique, values may repeat.

sections.title: Exception Handling
sections.content: Exception handling is used to manage runtime errors and keep programs from crashing. The main keywords are try, catch, finally, throw, and throws. try contains code that may generate an exception. catch handles it. finally runs whether or not an exception occurs, commonly used for cleanup. throw is used to explicitly throw an exception object, while throws is used in the method signature to declare that a method may throw exceptions. Checked exceptions are checked at compile time and must be handled or declared, such as IOException and SQLException. Unchecked exceptions are runtime exceptions such as NullPointerException, ArithmeticException, and ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException. The Exception class hierarchy starts with Throwable, which has Error and Exception as main branches. Error generally represents serious system-level problems and is not usually handled in application logic. Multiple catch blocks can be used for different exception types. More specific exceptions should be caught before more general ones. Custom exceptions can be created by extending Exception or RuntimeException. MCQ traps: finally usually executes even after return, but it may not run in extreme cases like JVM shutdown; throw creates an exception, throws declares it; checked vs unchecked is a very common exam topic.

sections.title: Multithreading & Concurrency
sections.content: A thread is a lightweight unit of execution. Java supports multithreading to perform multiple tasks concurrently. Threads can be created by extending the Thread class or by implementing the Runnable interface. The start() method creates a new thread and calls run() internally; calling run() directly does not start a new thread. Thread life cycle states include new, runnable, running, blocked/waiting, and terminated. Synchronization is used to control access to shared resources and prevent race conditions. A synchronized method or synchronized block allows only one thread to enter the critical section at a time. sleep() pauses a thread for a specified time. join() makes one thread wait for another thread to finish. wait(), notify(), and notifyAll() are used for inter-thread communication and must be called inside synchronized context. The Executor framework and thread pools are preferred for managing tasks efficiently instead of manually creating many threads. MCQ traps: start() vs run() is very important; threads can cause race conditions if shared data is not protected; synchronized improves safety but can reduce performance.
